heroOverview: Pyonephrosis is the accumulation of infected pus within an obstructed, dilated renal collecting system — effectively converting a kidney into a bag of infected fluid. The condition arises when an upstream obstruction, most often a kidney stone or a ureteral stricture, traps urine and allows bacteria to colonise the stagnant pool. Without urgent drainage, bacterial toxins spill into the bloodstream within hours and drive the patient toward urosepsis, which carries a mortality of 20-40% in elderly or immunocompromised individuals. Pyonephrosis is therefore a urological emergency: diagnosis relies on clinical suspicion combined with renal ultrasound or CT, and definitive management is percutaneous nephrostomy or ureteral stent placement to decompress the kidney, followed by targeted antibiotics and, once infection is controlled, definitive treatment of the obstruction.

keyStats.prevalence: Complicates 0.5-1% of hospitalised urinary tract infections; found in up to 10% of patients presenting with febrile obstructive uropathy (EAU, 2024)
keyStats.demographics: Most common in adults with urolithiasis, ureteral strictures, or malignancy; higher incidence in diabetic patients and immunocompromised adults
keyStats.avgAge: Peak incidence in adults aged 40-70 with pre-existing stone disease or urological obstruction
keyStats.globalCases: Precise global incidence is not well-characterised; stone disease affects roughly 10% of adults worldwide, placing a large population at risk

definition: Pyonephrosis (ICD-10: N13.6) is a suppurative destruction of the renal parenchyma and collecting system caused by bacterial infection in the setting of urinary obstruction and hydronephrosis. The term literally means 'pus in the kidney.' When ureteral or ureteropelvic junction obstruction prevents normal urine drainage — due to a calculus, urothelial tumour, extrinsic compression, stricture, or congenital anomaly — stagnant urine above the blockage becomes infected. Common causative organisms are Gram-negative Enterobacteriaceae: Escherichia coli, Klebsiella pneumoniae, and Proteus mirabilis dominate, with Enterococcus and Pseudomonas species common in hospital-acquired or catheter-related cases. The kidney fills with turbid, purulent urine or frank pus; parenchymal destruction proceeds rapidly and can render the kidney permanently non-functional within days. Pyonephrosis differs from simple pyelonephritis (which lacks obstruction) and from a simple perinephric abscess (which lies outside the collecting system), though both can coexist. It falls within the management domain of urology, with critical care and infectious disease input when sepsis is established. Mortality without drainage approaches 50%, making prompt recognition and intervention life-saving.

typesClassification.description: The most common form, where a calculus lodged in the ureter or ureteropelvic junction obstructs drainage; the stagnant urine becomes secondarily infected.

Urothelial cancer, cervical cancer, or retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy compresses the ureter externally, causing obstruction that becomes infected; prognosis is influenced by the underlying malignancy.
Benign ureteral strictures from prior surgery, radiation, or ischaemia obstruct drainage; these patients often have recurrent episodes if the stricture is not addressed.

Ureteropelvic junction obstruction or vesicoureteral reflux in children creates the anatomical substrate; infection superimposed on a congenitally dilated system produces pyonephrosis in infants and young children.

primarySymptoms: High fever above 38.5°C with rigors and shaking chills, reflecting systemic bacteraemia arising from the obstructed infected kidney.
Severe, constant flank or loin pain on the affected side — unlike the colicky waves of an uncomplicated stone, this pain is steady because the kidney is distended with pus.
Costovertebral angle tenderness on percussion, often exquisite, over the involved kidney.
Nausea and vomiting, which reflect systemic toxicity and sometimes peritoneal irritation if the infection spreads retroperitoneally.
Rapid deterioration with signs of sepsis: tachycardia above 100 beats per minute, hypotension, and reduced urine output.
Dysuria, urinary frequency, or haematuria if the obstruction is distal and urine can still reach the bladder, though these are often absent when obstruction is complete.
Abdominal guarding or a palpable flank mass in cases with markedly distended renal pelvis or retroperitoneal extension.
Cloudy or frankly purulent urine on the side of obstruction, though the urine passed from the bladder may appear normal if the contralateral kidney is unaffected.
Confusion, agitation, or altered consciousness in elderly or diabetic patients as sepsis progresses.
Rapid deterioration despite antibiotic therapy alone — failure to improve within 12-24 hours of starting antibiotics is a red flag pointing to obstruction requiring drainage.
earlyWarningSigns: A previously diagnosed kidney stone presenting with new fever, even low-grade, is pyonephrosis until proven otherwise.
Worsening flank pain that has changed character from intermittent colic to a constant, deep ache.
Unexpected deterioration in a patient being treated for a urinary infection while also having known urological obstruction.
Laboratory results showing rising creatinine and white cell count together in a patient with known stone or ureteral disease.
emergencySigns: Fever above 39°C with shaking chills and one-sided flank pain in anyone with known obstructive uropathy — requires immediate assessment.
Signs of septic shock: systolic blood pressure below 90 mmHg, heart rate above 120, altered mental status, and cold extremities.
Anuria or severe oliguria with known single kidney and suspected obstruction.
Failure to improve or clinical worsening within 12-24 hours of intravenous antibiotics, which mandates emergency renal decompression.
Radiological finding of hydronephrosis combined with clinical signs of infection — drain before awaiting culture results.

causes.description: A stone lodged in the ureter or ureteropelvic junction blocks drainage; stagnant urine above the stone becomes colonised by ascending bacteria, and pressure prevents the kidney from clearing the infection. This is the most common precipitant, accounting for 60-80% of cases in most series.

Benign strictures from prior surgery, instrumentation, radiation, or ischaemia partially or completely obstruct the ureter; chronically impaired drainage creates a hospitable environment for recurrent bacterial colonisation that eventually progresses to suppuration.

Transitional cell carcinoma within the ureter, or external compression by cervical, colorectal, or retroperitoneal cancers, obstructs ureteral flow; cancer-related immunosuppression and altered mucosa further increase susceptibility to ascending infection.

High-grade vesicoureteral reflux propels infected urine retrograde into the collecting system under high pressure; repeated episodes of infected reflux can produce pyonephrosis in children and in adults with untreated reflux, particularly when a degree of functional obstruction coexists.

A congenitally narrow ureteropelvic junction impairs drainage of the renal pelvis; when a urinary infection occurs in this anatomical context, bacteria cannot be washed out and rapidly proliferate, causing pyonephrosis.

Ureteral injury, ligation, or oedema following pelvic or abdominal surgery can obstruct the ureter; secondary infection converts the resulting hydronephrosis into pyonephrosis within days, particularly in patients who also received urinary catheters or broad-spectrum antibiotics that select resistant organisms.

riskFactors.description: Urolithiasis is by far the most common predisposing condition; stones obstruct the ureter and provide a surface for bacterial biofilm. Stone prevention with hydration and metabolic management reduces pyonephrosis risk.

Glycosuria provides a nutrient-rich medium for bacterial growth, and diabetic neuropathy can impair bladder emptying; diabetic patients have roughly 3-5 times the risk of complicated urinary infection.

Organ transplant recipients, patients on corticosteroids or chemotherapy, and those with HIV-related immunodeficiency mount attenuated inflammatory responses, allowing infections to become established and progress rapidly before clinical symptoms become apparent.
Ureteral strictures developing after ureteroscopy, nephrolithotomy, or pelvic surgery create persistent obstructive anatomy; bacteriuria superimposed on these strictures readily causes pyonephrosis.

Pelvic and retroperitoneal malignancies are a significant cause of obstructive uropathy in older adults; the combination of obstruction, immunosuppression from chemotherapy, and altered mucosa makes infection and pyonephrosis likely.
A history of frequent UTIs identifies patients whose urinary flora is repeatedly bacteriuric; in the context of any degree of obstruction, these bacteria can ascend and infect the collecting system.

Ureteropelvic junction obstruction, duplex collecting systems, and horseshoe kidney create anatomically impaired drainage that predisposes to infection and pyonephrosis, particularly in infants and children.
Women have a shorter urethra and higher rates of ascending UTI; when obstructive uropathy coexists, the greater bacterial inoculum increases pyonephrosis risk.

affectedDemographics: Adults aged 40-70 with a history of kidney stone disease
Women with ureteral obstruction, given their higher baseline UTI incidence
Diabetic individuals with recurrent urinary infections
Children with congenital ureteropelvic junction obstruction or high-grade vesicoureteral reflux
Immunocompromised adults including transplant recipients and oncology patients
diagnosisOverview: The clinical picture of fever, rigors, and flank pain in a patient with known or suspected obstructive uropathy should prompt immediate imaging rather than a trial of antibiotics alone. Renal ultrasound is the fastest and most accessible first test: it reliably shows hydronephrosis and, in about 50-70% of cases, reveals echogenic fluid or debris within the collecting system suggesting pus. However, a normal-looking fluid collection on ultrasound does not exclude pyonephrosis — urine and pus can look identical on grey-scale imaging. Non-contrast CT of the abdomen and pelvis is the gold-standard investigation: it identifies the level and cause of obstruction, demonstrates peri-renal fat stranding, gas within the collecting system (a marker of emphysematous infection), and loss of renal parenchymal enhancement if the kidney is destroyed. Blood tests — full blood count, CRP, procalcitonin, creatinine, and electrolytes — quantify the inflammatory burden and renal function. Blood cultures should be drawn before antibiotics in every febrile patient. Urine microscopy and culture provide microbiological confirmation; in complete obstruction the voided urine may appear normal because it comes from the unaffected contralateral kidney, so percutaneous nephrostomy fluid or ureteral catheter drainage provides the definitive specimen. The key differential is acute pyelonephritis without obstruction: in uncomplicated pyelonephritis, imaging shows an enlarged inflamed kidney without significant dilatation, and it responds to antibiotics alone. Any febrile patient whose UTI is not responding to antibiotics within 24-48 hours should be imaged urgently to exclude obstruction.

diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line rapid assessment to confirm hydronephrosis and look for echogenic debris or pus within the dilated collecting system.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A probe is pressed over the flank with gel; the scan takes 10-20 minutes and produces no radiation. It reliably shows obstruction but cannot always distinguish pus from clear fluid.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold-standard imaging that identifies the site and cause of obstruction, detects peri-renal inflammation, and reveals emphysematous or haemorrhagic complications.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You lie still in a doughnut-shaped scanner for about 5-10 minutes; no contrast is given initially in pyonephrosis because contrast can obscure stones and delay drainage decisions.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the causative organism and antibiotic sensitivities when bacteria have entered the bloodstream, guiding definitive antibiotic therapy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Two sets of blood are drawn from separate arm veins before antibiotics start; results take 24-72 hours but early empirical antibiotics are started immediately.

diagnosticTests.test: Full blood count, CRP, procalcitonin, creatinine
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies the severity of systemic infection, monitors renal function, and helps guide the need for intensive care admission.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A single blood draw; results are usually available within 1-4 hours in an emergency department.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the uropathogen and confirms pyuria; particularly informative on nephrostomy drainage fluid rather than voided urine if the obstruction is complete.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A mid-stream urine sample is collected before antibiotics; culture results take 24-48 hours. In complete obstruction, nephrostomy fluid is sent as the definitive sample.

diagnosticTests.test: Nephrostomy fluid Gram stain and culture
diagnosticTests.purpose: Provides the most direct microbiological specimen from the infected collecting system, often positive even when blood and urine cultures are negative.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Fluid aspirated at the time of percutaneous nephrostomy is sent immediately to microbiology; Gram stain results return within an hour and confirm the diagnosis.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Performed after infection is controlled to quantify residual renal function and decide whether the kidney can be salvaged or requires nephrectomy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small amount of radioactive tracer is injected intravenously; a camera captures images over 2-3 hours. Used in planning, not in the acute emergency phase.

treatmentOverview: Treatment is a true urological emergency: antibiotics alone cannot clear infection behind an obstruction. The therapeutic sequence is drainage first, antibiotics second, definitive stone or stricture management third. Broad-spectrum intravenous antibiotics — a third- or fourth-generation cephalosporin, piperacillin-tazobactam, or a carbapenem in healthcare-associated cases — are started immediately but serve as a holding measure until the obstruction is relieved. Two drainage options exist: percutaneous nephrostomy (PCN), in which a radiologist places a tube through the flank directly into the renal pelvis under ultrasound guidance, and retrograde ureteral stent insertion by a urologist using a cystoscope. Both achieve decompression, but PCN is generally preferred when the patient is septic or the stone burden is large, because it is faster to perform under local anaesthesia and directly drains the pus rather than pushing past the obstruction. Purulent nephrostomy output confirms the diagnosis at placement. After drainage and clinical stabilisation — typically 48-72 hours of intravenous antibiotics followed by oral step-down — definitive treatment of the underlying obstruction is planned: ureteroscopy with laser lithotripsy or shockwave lithotripsy for stones, endoscopic balloon dilation or surgical repair for strictures, and stenting or nephrectomy for malignancy-related obstruction. Nephrectomy may be required if the kidney has been destroyed or is non-functional on post-infection scintigraphy. The total antibiotic course is typically 10-14 days, culture-directed where possible. Septic patients require intensive-care monitoring, vasopressors if blood pressure is not maintained, and renal replacement therapy if acute kidney injury supervenes.

medicalTreatments.name: Broad-spectrum intravenous antibiotics
medicalTreatments.description: Empirical IV therapy with a third-generation cephalosporin (e.g. ceftriaxone), piperacillin-tazobactam, or carbapenem is started immediately based on the likely organisms and local resistance patterns; the regimen is narrowed once culture and sensitivity results return at 24-48 hours.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Antibiotics alone cannot cure pyonephrosis when obstruction persists, but early appropriate antibiotics reduce mortality by roughly 30-40% compared to delayed or inadequate therapy in septic patients; survival depends primarily on drainage.

medicalTreatments.name: Percutaneous nephrostomy (PCN)
medicalTreatments.description: A radiologist places a drainage catheter directly into the renal pelvis under ultrasound or fluoroscopic guidance under local anaesthesia; purulent urine drains immediately, relieving pressure and reducing the bacterial load. PCN is preferred in septic patients, in those with large proximal stones, and in patients who cannot tolerate general anaesthesia.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical improvement is observed within 24-48 hours in approximately 85-95% of patients when performed promptly; it is the definitive decompression step in pyonephrosis management.

medicalTreatments.name: Retrograde ureteral stent insertion
medicalTreatments.description: A urologist passes a flexible JJ-stent cystoscopically past the obstruction under sedation or general anaesthesia to restore urine drainage from above; this is an alternative to PCN and preferred when the stone can be bypassed or when there is no interventional radiology access.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Achieves decompression in 80-90% of cases; initial failure rate is higher than PCN for large impacted stones because passage of the guidewire past the stone may not be possible.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral antibiotic step-down and completion
medicalTreatments.description: Once the patient is afebrile and clinically improving, IV antibiotics are switched to an oral agent guided by culture sensitivities (commonly ciprofloxacin or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole); the total course is usually 10-14 days.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Oral step-down after 48-72 hours of IV therapy is supported by randomised data and reduces hospital length of stay without compromising cure rates compared to prolonged IV courses.

medicalTreatments.name: Sepsis resuscitation (IV fluids, vasopressors)
medicalTreatments.description: In patients with septic shock, aggressive fluid resuscitation following Surviving Sepsis Campaign bundles — 30 mL/kg crystalloid within the first hour — and norepinephrine for persistent hypotension are standard alongside drainage.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Adherence to hour-1 sepsis bundles reduces in-hospital mortality by approximately 25% in studies of obstructive urosepsis.

surgicalOptions.name: Ureteroscopy with laser lithotripsy
surgicalOptions.description: After infection is fully controlled (usually 2-6 weeks post-drainage), a urologist passes a flexible ureteroscope to the stone and uses a holmium laser to fragment and remove it, treating the obstructing cause definitively.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Stone-free rates of 85-95% for ureteral stones under 15 mm in experienced centres.

surgicalOptions.name: Percutaneous nephrolithotomy (PCNL)
surgicalOptions.description: For large renal stones (>20 mm) or staghorn calculi causing pyonephrosis, PCNL removes stone burden through a nephroscope inserted percutaneously once infection has cleared.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Stone-free rate of approximately 80-90% for large stones; a staged approach after infection control is standard.

surgicalOptions.name: Nephrectomy
surgicalOptions.description: When the infected kidney has been functionally destroyed (less than 10-15% split function on DMSA scan) or when pyonephrosis is associated with an underlying malignancy making salvage impossible, nephrectomy removes the source. It can be performed laparoscopically in most cases once infection is controlled.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Removes the source of recurrent sepsis; post-operative mortality is low (<2%) in centres performing the procedure after acute infection is treated.

surgicalOptions.name: Endoscopic ureteral balloon dilation or reconstructive surgery
surgicalOptions.description: For benign strictures causing recurrent pyonephrosis, endoscopic dilation, ureteroscopic incision, or open/laparoscopic ureteral reconstruction (ureteroplasty, Boari flap) restores drainage permanently.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Long-term patency rates of 60-85% depending on stricture length and location.

alternativeTreatments.name: Increased fluid intake and urinary alkalization
alternativeTreatments.description: In patients with uric-acid stones specifically, oral urinary alkalization with potassium citrate can dissolve the stone over weeks; this is a non-invasive adjunct but is too slow for acute pyonephrosis and only applicable after the acute infection is resolved.

alternativeTreatments.name: Metabolic stone prevention
alternativeTreatments.description: After definitive stone treatment, evidence-based dietary modifications — increased water intake to over 2.5 litres per day, reduced sodium and animal protein, and specific pharmacotherapy based on 24-hour urine metabolic analysis — lower the 5-year stone recurrence rate by approximately 50% and thereby reduce the lifetime risk of recurrent pyonephrosis.

whySeeSpecialist: Pyonephrosis is a urological emergency that requires immediate urologist involvement for drainage planning and intervention. Even if the patient is admitted under general medicine or emergency medicine, urology must be consulted without delay. A urologist determines which drainage modality — percutaneous nephrostomy or ureteral stent — is appropriate, performs or coordinates the procedure, and then plans the definitive treatment of the underlying obstruction once infection is controlled. Delays in specialist involvement measurably worsen outcomes.
doctorSelectionGuide: For the acute presentation, any urologist with access to interventional radiology is appropriate. For definitive stone management, look for a urologist with endourology subspecialty training and high-volume experience in ureteroscopy and PCNL. For malignancy-related obstruction, oncology, urology, and interventional radiology work together. Ask about the unit's 24-hour access to percutaneous nephrostomy and their protocol for managing obstructive urosepsis.
preventionStrategies: Maintain fluid intake above 2-2.5 litres per day to dilute urine and reduce stone formation.
Treat kidney stones promptly — an obstructing stone in the ureter should not be left to pass spontaneously if there is any sign of infection.
Seek same-day evaluation for fever or rigors in anyone with a known ureteral stone or history of ureteral stricture.
Follow a metabolic stone workup after a first stone to identify and correct correctable risk factors (hypercalciuria, hyperuricosuria, low citrate).
Manage diabetes tightly — optimal glycaemic control reduces complicated urinary infection risk.
Ensure follow-up imaging after any episode of obstructive uropathy to confirm the kidney has drained and the obstruction has resolved.
lifestyleModifications: Drink enough water to produce at least 2 litres of urine per day — the single most effective stone prevention measure.
Reduce dietary sodium to below 2,300 mg per day and limit animal protein, both of which increase urinary calcium and uric acid.
Maintain a healthy weight, as obesity increases stone risk and inflammatory response.
Avoid prolonged dehydration during exercise or in hot weather, which concentrates urine and promotes crystal formation.
Report any recurrence of flank pain or fever immediately rather than waiting to see if it resolves on its own.
If you have known obstructive anatomy, carry a medical alert card listing your urological history so emergency providers can act quickly.

recommended: Water as the primary fluid, targeting 2-2.5 litres of urine output per day
Citrus juices such as lemon or lime water, which increase urinary citrate and inhibit calcium stone formation
Moderate dairy intake to maintain normal calcium levels, as low dietary calcium paradoxically increases oxalate absorption
Vegetables and whole grains as the basis of diet — a plant-forward diet is associated with lower stone recurrence
avoid: High-sodium foods (processed meats, canned soups, fast food) that raise urinary calcium excretion
Excessive animal protein from red meat, fish, or poultry, which raises urinary uric acid and lowers pH
High-oxalate foods such as spinach, nuts, and chocolate in excess if you have calcium-oxalate stones
Sugary drinks and concentrated fructose, which increase uric acid production and stone risk

exerciseGuidelines: Moderate regular exercise is encouraged for general health and to maintain normal body weight, as obesity is an independent stone risk factor. During or following an episode of pyonephrosis, rest is appropriate until fever resolves and the nephrostomy (if present) is secure. Vigorous exercise that risks dislodging a nephrostomy tube should be avoided until the tube is removed. After full recovery, there are no activity restrictions; patients are encouraged to return to normal activity and to stay well hydrated during exercise.
dailyManagement: If a nephrostomy tube is in place, check the drainage bag each day for colour, volume, and odour; cloudy or bloody output should be reported.
Take oral antibiotics at the same time each day and complete the full course even when feeling well.
Monitor temperature daily during recovery — a return of fever above 38°C warrants same-day medical contact.
Keep follow-up urology and radiology appointments; drainage tube exchanges are needed every 4-8 weeks if the tube remains.
Document all stone-passing episodes with timing and side to help the urologist track stone activity.
If metabolic stone investigations have been ordered, complete the 24-hour urine collection under standard dietary conditions as instructed.
prognosis: With prompt drainage and appropriate antibiotics, the majority of patients with pyonephrosis survive and recover from the acute episode; reported in-hospital mortality ranges from 2-5% in series with rapid decompression to 20-40% when septic shock is established or drainage is delayed. Renal salvage depends on how quickly drainage is achieved: kidneys drained within 24 hours of presentation recover split function in most cases, while those obstructed for more than 72 hours before drainage often sustain irreversible parenchymal loss. Approximately 20-30% of kidneys affected by severe pyonephrosis show significant functional impairment on post-infection nuclear scintigraphy, and a subset require nephrectomy. Recurrence depends entirely on the underlying cause: stone formers who receive metabolic evaluation and prevention advice have a significantly lower recurrence rate, while patients with malignancy-related obstruction face ongoing risk tied to the cancer's behaviour. Long-term kidney function in the contralateral kidney is usually preserved unless bilateral disease or underlying chronic kidney disease is present.
recoveryTimeline: Fever and systemic toxicity typically begin to improve within 12-24 hours of effective drainage. Most patients become afebrile within 48-72 hours and can be transitioned from intravenous to oral antibiotics. Hospitalisation averages 5-10 days for the acute episode. The nephrostomy tube, if placed, usually stays in place for 2-6 weeks until infection is completely controlled and the definitive stone procedure is scheduled. After ureteroscopic stone removal, most patients can resume normal activities within 1-2 weeks. Full functional recovery of the kidney takes 4-12 weeks and is confirmed by follow-up imaging.
complications: Urosepsis and septic shock — bacterial translocation from the obstructed kidney into the bloodstream; the leading cause of death in pyonephrosis, particularly in diabetic and immunocompromised patients.
Acute kidney injury — reduced renal perfusion from sepsis combined with direct parenchymal destruction can cause acute-on-chronic kidney disease requiring temporary dialysis.
Permanent renal functional loss — irreversible parenchymal destruction if drainage is delayed or if infection recurs repeatedly in the same kidney.
Perinephric abscess extension — pus erodes through the renal capsule into the perinephric fat or retroperitoneum, requiring separate drainage and a more prolonged antibiotic course.
Emphysematous pyelonephritis — a particularly severe form where gas-forming organisms produce gas within the renal parenchyma, carrying mortality above 40% if not recognised and managed aggressively.
Nephrectomy and loss of the kidney — when pyonephrosis is diagnosed late or is recurrent and the kidney is non-functional on scintigraphy, surgical removal is the safest option.

supportResources.name: NIDDK — Kidney Disease and Urologic Health
supportResources.description: Patient-facing resources on kidney and urinary tract conditions from the U.S. National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases.

supportResources.name: European Association of Urology (EAU) Patient Information
supportResources.description: Evidence-based patient guides on urological conditions including infections and kidney stone disease, produced from the EAU's clinical guidelines.

supportResources.name: NHS — Kidney infections
supportResources.description: UK NHS patient information on kidney infections including when to seek emergency care.

sources.title: Shokeir AA et al. Percutaneous nephrostomy vs ureteral stents for obstructed, infected hydronephrosis. World Journal of Urology, 2008
Berrouane YF et al. Pyonephrosis: impact of bacteraemia, APACHE II score, and site of obstruction. Journal of Hospital Infection, 2000
Kalra OP, Raizada A. Approach to a patient with urosepsis. Journal of Global Infectious Diseases, 2009
Surviving Sepsis Campaign International Guidelines for Management of Sepsis and Septic Shock 2021
NICE NG118 — Renal and ureteral stones: assessment and management

confusedWithConditions.name: Urinary tract infection
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: A UTI without obstruction produces dysuria and frequency without hydronephrosis on imaging and responds to antibiotics alone; pyonephrosis requires drainage and involves a dilated, obstructed, pus-filled collecting system.

confusedWithConditions.name: Kidney stones (nephrolithiasis)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: An obstructing stone alone causes colicky flank pain without fever or systemic toxicity; once infection supervenes with fever and rigors, the diagnosis becomes pyonephrosis and emergency drainage is required.

confusedWithConditions.name: Vesicoureteral reflux
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Vesicoureteral reflux causes reflux of bladder urine into the ureter and is diagnosed on voiding cystourethrogram; when reflux is severe and infected, it can produce pyonephrosis but reflux itself is managed differently from obstructive pyonephrosis.

relatedConditions.name: Nephrolithiasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Obstructing kidney stones are the most common cause of pyonephrosis; stone disease and pyonephrosis share patients, management pathways, and prevention strategies.

relatedConditions.name: Urinary tract infection
relatedConditions.relevance: Pyonephrosis is an ascending, obstructed UTI; understanding the spectrum from simple cystitis to pyonephrosis is essential for appropriate escalation of care.

relatedConditions.name: Vesicoureteral reflux
relatedConditions.relevance: High-grade reflux in children and adults is a recognised anatomical substrate for pyonephrosis when superimposed on infection.

relatedConditions.name: Prostate cancer
relatedConditions.relevance: Locally advanced prostate cancer can obstruct the ureter, creating the substrate for obstructive uropathy and pyonephrosis.

faqs.question: What is pyonephrosis?
faqs.answer: Pyonephrosis is a kidney filled with pus because an obstruction — usually a kidney stone or stricture — has blocked urine drainage and allowed bacteria to infect the trapped fluid. It is a urological emergency requiring urgent drainage.

faqs.question: How is pyonephrosis different from a kidney infection (pyelonephritis)?
faqs.answer: Pyelonephritis is a bacterial kidney infection without obstruction and can be cured with antibiotics alone. Pyonephrosis occurs when obstruction traps infected urine as pus inside the kidney; antibiotics cannot clear it without drainage, making pyonephrosis far more dangerous.

faqs.question: What causes pyonephrosis?
faqs.answer: The most common cause is an obstructing kidney stone lodged in the ureter. Other causes include ureteral strictures, cancers compressing the ureter, congenital narrowing at the ureteropelvic junction, and severe vesicoureteral reflux combined with infection.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of pyonephrosis?
faqs.answer: The main symptoms are high fever with rigors, severe constant flank pain on the affected side, nausea, and rapid deterioration. Some patients develop signs of sepsis such as low blood pressure and confusion. Unlike a simple kidney stone, the pain does not come in waves — it is constant.

faqs.question: Is pyonephrosis a medical emergency?
faqs.answer: Yes. Without drainage, infected pus in the kidney drives bacteria into the bloodstream within hours, causing septic shock with a mortality of 20-40% in high-risk patients. Any patient with fever and a known or suspected obstructed kidney needs same-day emergency assessment.

faqs.question: How is pyonephrosis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis uses clinical suspicion combined with imaging. Renal ultrasound shows hydronephrosis and sometimes debris in the collecting system. CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis gives the most detail, identifying the obstruction level and any complications such as gas in the kidney. Blood and urine cultures confirm the organism.

faqs.question: How is pyonephrosis treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment starts with intravenous antibiotics and urgent drainage of the kidney — either by percutaneous nephrostomy (a tube through the flank placed by a radiologist) or a ureteral stent inserted by a urologist. Drainage must happen within hours of diagnosis. Definitive treatment of the cause, such as stone removal, follows once infection is controlled.

faqs.question: What is a percutaneous nephrostomy?
faqs.answer: A percutaneous nephrostomy is a small drainage tube inserted through the skin of the flank directly into the kidney under ultrasound guidance. It drains pus immediately, relieving pressure and clearing the infection. The procedure is done under local anaesthetic and takes about 30 minutes.

faqs.question: Can pyonephrosis be treated with antibiotics alone?
faqs.answer: No. Antibiotics are essential but cannot cure pyonephrosis because the obstruction prevents the infected fluid from draining. Without mechanical drainage (nephrostomy or ureteral stent), bacteria continue to multiply and sepsis progresses despite the antibiotic course.

faqs.question: Will my kidney recover after pyonephrosis?
faqs.answer: Most kidneys recover meaningful function if drainage is achieved quickly — within 24-48 hours of the obstruction becoming infected. Kidneys drained after longer delays can suffer permanent loss of function. About 20-30% of severely affected kidneys show lasting impairment; a small proportion need to be removed.

faqs.question: Can pyonephrosis cause kidney failure?
faqs.answer: Yes, in two ways. First, sepsis reduces blood flow to the kidneys causing acute kidney injury. Second, the infected kidney itself can be destroyed by the infection. If the other kidney is healthy, total kidney failure is uncommon, but temporary dialysis may be needed in severe cases.

faqs.question: Does pyonephrosis require surgery?
faqs.answer: Drainage by nephrostomy or stent is a minor procedure, not major surgery. However, once infection is controlled, surgery to remove the stone — ureteroscopy or PCNL — is usually needed. If the kidney is functionally destroyed by the time treatment starts, nephrectomy (surgical kidney removal) may be recommended.

faqs.question: How long is recovery from pyonephrosis?
faqs.answer: Fever usually resolves within 48-72 hours of drainage. Hospital stays average 5-10 days. A nephrostomy tube typically remains for 2-6 weeks until the cause is treated definitively. Full recovery, including stone treatment if needed, takes 4-12 weeks.

faqs.question: Can pyonephrosis come back?
faqs.answer: It can recur if the underlying cause is not treated. Stone formers who do not receive prevention advice and metabolic evaluation face ongoing risk. Patients with strictures or malignancy-related obstruction also remain at risk for recurrence. Treating the root cause is the key to preventing another episode.

faqs.question: What is the difference between pyonephrosis and a perinephric abscess?
faqs.answer: In pyonephrosis, pus is trapped inside the kidney's collecting system. In a perinephric abscess, pus collects in the fat surrounding the kidney, outside the renal capsule. They can coexist, but perinephric abscess may need separate drainage and suggests the infection has extended beyond the kidney.

faqs.question: How is pyonephrosis prevented?
faqs.answer: Prevention centres on avoiding the obstructions that cause it. For stone formers, drinking 2-2.5 litres of water daily and following metabolic stone prevention advice reduces stone recurrence by about 50%. Any fever in a person with a known kidney stone should be assessed urgently, as early treatment prevents pyonephrosis.

faqs.question: Who is most at risk of pyonephrosis?
faqs.answer: People with kidney stones are at highest risk. Diabetic patients, immunocompromised individuals, those with prior ureteral surgery or strictures, and patients with pelvic malignancies are also at elevated risk. Elderly patients tend to present later and have worse outcomes because their symptoms can be atypical.

faqs.question: Is pyonephrosis the same as emphysematous pyelonephritis?
faqs.answer: No, though both are severe kidney infections. Emphysematous pyelonephritis is a necrotising infection where gas-forming bacteria produce gas within the kidney parenchyma itself, not just in the collecting system. It is diagnosed on CT, predominantly affects diabetics, and carries even higher mortality — above 40% — without aggressive surgery or nephrectomy.

faqs.question: What bacteria cause pyonephrosis?
faqs.answer: The most common organisms are Gram-negative bacteria from the gut: Escherichia coli (most frequent), Klebsiella pneumoniae, and Proteus mirabilis. In hospitalised or catheterised patients, Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Enterococcus species are more common. Culture of the nephrostomy drainage fluid gives the definitive answer.

faqs.question: Can a child get pyonephrosis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Children with congenital ureteropelvic junction obstruction or high-grade vesicoureteral reflux are at risk of pyonephrosis when a urinary infection occurs in the setting of impaired drainage. Febrile UTI in a child with known congenital uropathy warrants prompt imaging to exclude pyonephrosis.

metaDescription: Pyonephrosis is pus trapped in an obstructed kidney — a urological emergency. Learn symptoms, causes, drainage treatment and recovery. Doctor-reviewed.
